subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In isometric projection, the three isometric axes are equally inclined to the plane of projection. The angle between any two isometric axes is:
explanation: In isometric projection, the three axes make equal angles of 120° with each other. Two axes make 30° with the horizontal, and the third is vertical.

elimination_hints.hint: 90° is the angle between axes in orthographic projection, not isometric.
60° is half of 120°. The supplementary angle is not correct; the inter-axis angle is 120°.
150° is the angle between an isometric axis and its extension, not between two different axes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using supplementary instead of inter-axis angle

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The isometric scale (ratio of isometric length to true length) is approximately:
explanation: The isometric scale = √(2/3) ≈ 0.8165. This means every true length is reduced to about 81.65% in isometric projection.

elimination_hints.hint: 1.0 means no reduction. This would be an isometric drawing (using true lengths), not an isometric projection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing isometric drawing with isometric projection

elimination_hints.hint: 0.5 is too much reduction. The isometric scale is √(2/3) ≈ 0.816, not 0.5.
1.22 ≈ √(3/2) is the inverse of the isometric scale. It would enlarge, not reduce.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The key difference between an isometric drawing and an isometric projection is:
explanation: An isometric DRAWING uses true (actual) dimensions directly on the isometric axes — it's simpler and commonly used. An isometric PROJECTION uses the isometric scale (√(2/3) ≈ 0.816) to reduce all dimensions, giving the mathematically correct projection.

options.text: Isometric drawing uses true lengths; isometric projection uses the isometric scale (×0.816)
Isometric drawing is 2D; isometric projection is 3D
They are exactly the same thing
Isometric projection uses true lengths; isometric drawing uses reduced lengths

elimination_hints.hint: Both are 2D representations of 3D objects. The difference is in scale, not dimensionality.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing representation type with scale difference

elimination_hints.hint: They are NOT the same. Isometric drawing is larger (true scale) than isometric projection (reduced scale).
This is reversed. Isometric DRAWING uses true lengths. Isometric PROJECTION uses the reduced scale.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Lines that are parallel to the isometric axes are called:
explanation: Lines parallel to any of the three isometric axes are called isometric lines. They can be directly measured using the isometric scale. Lines not parallel to any isometric axis are non-isometric lines.

elimination_hints.hint: Orthographic lines relate to orthographic projection, not specifically to isometric axes.
Non-isometric lines are lines that are NOT parallel to any isometric axis. This is the opposite of what's asked.
Oblique lines are a general term for inclined lines. The specific term for lines parallel to isometric axes is 'isometric lines'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using general term instead of specific

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In an isometric view, a rectangular face of a solid appears as:
explanation: In isometric view, the axes are at 120° (not 90°). Rectangular faces appear as parallelograms because the right angles of the rectangle are distorted by the isometric projection.

elimination_hints.hint: A rectangle has 90° angles. In isometric view, the angles become 60° and 120°, forming a parallelogram.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming angles are preserved in isometric

elimination_hints.hint: Circles appear as ellipses in isometric, not as the projection of rectangles.
A triangle has 3 sides. A rectangle (4 sides) projects as a parallelogram (4 sides), not a triangle.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: A circle drawn on any face of an isometric view appears as:
explanation: In isometric view, all planes are inclined to the projection plane. A circle on an isometric plane projects as an ellipse. The 4-center approximate method is commonly used to draw these isometric ellipses.

elimination_hints.hint: A circle appears as a circle only when the plane containing it is parallel to the projection plane. In isometric, no plane is parallel to the projection plane.
A straight line would mean the circle's plane is perpendicular to the viewing direction. In isometric, planes are inclined, not perpendicular.
A parabola is a conic section. The projection of a circle on an inclined plane is always an ellipse, never a parabola.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Non-isometric lines (lines not parallel to any isometric axis) in an isometric view:
explanation: Non-isometric lines (diagonals, inclined edges not parallel to axes) cannot be scaled directly. They are drawn by first locating their endpoints using isometric coordinates, then connecting them.

options.text: Can be measured directly using the isometric scale
Cannot be measured directly and must be located by their endpoints
Are never drawn in isometric views

elimination_hints.hint: Only isometric lines (parallel to axes) can be measured directly. Non-isometric lines are foreshortened differently.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying isometric scale to non-isometric lines

elimination_hints.hint: Non-isometric lines do appear in isometric views (e.g., diagonals of faces, inclined surfaces). They just can't be measured directly.
Non-isometric lines can be in any direction. 'Non-isometric' means not parallel to any axis, not necessarily vertical.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing non-isometric with a specific direction

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: When constructing the isometric view of an object, the most common approach is the:
explanation: The box method involves enclosing the object in a rectangular box whose edges are along the isometric axes. The isometric view of this box is drawn first, then the object's features are located within it.

elimination_hints.hint: The sectional method is for drawing sections of solids, not for constructing isometric views.
Auxiliary views are used in orthographic projection to find true shapes of inclined surfaces.
Development is for unfolding surfaces, not for drawing isometric views.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The 4-center approximate method is used in isometric drawing to draw:
explanation: The 4-center method constructs an approximate ellipse using four circular arcs. It is used to represent circles that appear on isometric planes (top, front, or side faces) as ellipses.

options.text: Approximate ellipses representing circles on isometric planes

elimination_hints.hint: Straight edges along axes are drawn directly. No special method is needed for straight isometric lines.
Hidden lines are drawn as dashed lines. The 4-center method is specifically for circular features.
The isometric scale is a ratio (√(2/3) ≈ 0.816). It's not something that's 'drawn' using the 4-center method.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The isometric view of a right circular cylinder standing upright consists of:
explanation: The top and bottom circular faces appear as ellipses in isometric view. The two outermost generators (tangent to both ellipses) form the visible lateral edges, connecting the two ellipses.

options.text: Two ellipses connected by two tangent lines

elimination_hints.hint: Circles appear as ellipses in isometric, not as circles. The faces are parallelograms, not rectangles.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming shapes are preserved in isometric

elimination_hints.hint: A rectangle is the front/side view in orthographic projection. Isometric shows the 3D appearance with elliptical faces.
A single ellipse represents only one face. The cylinder has two circular faces (both visible as ellipses) and a curved surface.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: An isometric plane is a plane that:
explanation: An isometric plane is any plane that contains two of the three isometric axes (or is parallel to such a plane). There are three isometric planes: XY, YZ, and XZ. Circles on these planes appear as ellipses.

options.text: Is parallel to the projection plane
Is perpendicular to all three isometric axes

elimination_hints.hint: The projection plane is not an isometric plane. Isometric planes are inclined to the projection plane.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing projection plane with isometric plane

elimination_hints.hint: No plane can be perpendicular to all three axes simultaneously (since they span 3D space). An isometric plane contains two axes.
Isometric planes contain isometric lines (parallel to their two axes). Non-isometric lines may also lie in isometric planes.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: A cube has a true edge length of 50 mm. In an isometric projection, each edge measures:
explanation: Isometric length = True length × isometric scale = 50 × √(2/3) = 50 × 0.8165 = 40.82 mm.

elimination_hints.hint: 50 mm is the true length. In isometric projection (not drawing), the scale factor reduces this.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using true length instead of isometric projection length

elimination_hints.hint: 61.24 mm = 50 × √(3/2) is the inverse. This would enlarge, not reduce.
35 mm = 50 × 0.7. The correct factor is 0.8165 (√(2/3)), not 0.7.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In standard isometric projection, the direction of viewing is along the:
explanation: Isometric projection views the object along the body diagonal of an imaginary cube surrounding the object. This direction is equally inclined to all three principal axes (at approximately 35.26° to each), making the three axes appear equally foreshortened.

options.text: Body diagonal of a cube (equally inclined to all three axes)
One of the edges of the cube

elimination_hints.hint: Viewing along a cube edge gives an orthographic view (one face flat), not isometric.
Viewing along a face diagonal gives a dimetric view (two axes equal, one different), not isometric.
Perpendicular to one face gives a standard orthographic view. Isometric requires equal inclination to all three axes.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: A right angle (90°) between two edges of a solid appears in an isometric view as:
explanation: In isometric view, right angles between isometric lines appear as either 60° or 120° (since the isometric axes are at 120° to each other). The actual 90° is never preserved in the isometric projection.

options.text: Either 60° or 120° (if edges are along isometric axes)

elimination_hints.hint: 90° is not preserved in isometric projection. The axes are at 120°, so right angles become 60° or 120°.
45° doesn't correspond to any standard angle in isometric. The axes produce 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150° angles.
180° means a straight line. Two perpendicular edges would never appear collinear in isometric.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing straight line with right angle

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The isometric view of a sphere is:
explanation: A sphere looks the same from every direction. In isometric projection, it appears as a circle. However, in isometric PROJECTION (with scale 0.816), the circle's diameter = true diameter × (major axis scale). In practice, a sphere is drawn as a circle of the true diameter.

options.text: A circle with the same diameter as the sphere
A circle with diameter equal to the major axis of the isometric ellipse of a great circle
A circle with the same diameter as the sphere

elimination_hints.hint: An ellipse is the projection of a circle on an inclined plane. A sphere has no flat faces — it projects as a circle from any direction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing sphere with circular face on a flat surface

elimination_hints.hint: In true isometric projection, the sphere diameter is scaled. But in standard practice (isometric drawing), the sphere is drawn as a circle of true diameter.
elimination_hints.misconception: Slight nuance; in standard practice both B and D say the same thing

elimination_hints.hint: The major axis of an isometric ellipse is larger than the true diameter. Using it would draw the sphere too large.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Compared to oblique projection, the advantage of isometric projection is that:
explanation: In isometric projection, all three axes are equally foreshortened, giving a balanced, realistic appearance. In oblique projection, one face shows true shape but the receding axis is distorted, making the view less natural.

options.text: Isometric gives a more realistic appearance because all three axes are equally foreshortened
Isometric always shows all six faces

elimination_hints.hint: Oblique projection is actually easier (one face drawn as true shape). Isometric's advantage is realism, not ease.
No pictorial projection preserves true angles. All angles are distorted in both isometric and oblique.
Maximum 3 faces are visible in any single isometric view. Hidden faces require separate views.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: To draw an inclined surface (not parallel to any isometric plane) in isometric view, you should:
explanation: Inclined surfaces produce non-isometric lines. These cannot be drawn directly. Instead, locate each endpoint of the inclined edge using its X, Y, Z coordinates in the isometric system, then connect the points.

options.text: Draw it as a straight line
Locate the endpoints of the inclined edges using isometric coordinates and connect them
Ignore it since it cannot be shown

elimination_hints.hint: A straight line may be part of the answer, but the key issue is HOW to locate the endpoints. Direct measurement doesn't work for inclined surfaces.
Inclined surfaces are common and must be shown. They are drawn by endpoint location, not omitted.
The 4-center method is for drawing circles/ellipses on isometric planes, not for inclined surfaces.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ellipse method with surface construction

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In an isometric view, circles on the top face, front face, and side face of a cube appear as ellipses. The major axis of the ellipse on the top face is oriented:
explanation: On the top face (horizontal isometric plane), the major axis of the ellipse is horizontal. On the front face, the major axis is at 60° to horizontal (perpendicular to the left isometric axis). On the side face, it's at 60° the other way.

elimination_hints.hint: The vertical major axis corresponds to a face not visible as a standard top face. The top face ellipse has a horizontal major axis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing top face with side face orientation

elimination_hints.hint: 30° is the angle of the isometric axes with horizontal, not the major axis orientation. The major axis is perpendicular to the 'missing' axis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing axis angle with major axis

elimination_hints.hint: 60° is the major axis orientation for front and side face ellipses, not the top face.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using front/side face angle for top face

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: When converting orthographic views to an isometric view, the most critical step is:
explanation: The crucial step is reading the orthographic views to extract L, W, H and then mapping them correctly: one dimension along each isometric axis. Misidentifying or misplacing dimensions leads to an incorrect isometric view.

options.text: Correctly identifying the three dimensions (length, width, height) from the orthographic views and mapping them to the three isometric axes
Using a compass to draw circles

elimination_hints.hint: Drawing axes is important but mechanical. The critical intellectual step is correctly reading and mapping dimensions from ortho views.
Compass use is a drafting technique. The conceptual challenge is interpreting the orthographic views correctly.
Hidden lines are drawn last, after the visible geometry. The critical step is understanding the shape from ortho views.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In a dimetric projection, unlike isometric projection:
explanation: Dimetric projection has two axes equally foreshortened and the third different. Isometric has all three equal. Trimetric has all three different. These are the three types of axonometric projection.

options.text: All three axes are equally foreshortened
Two axes are equally foreshortened and the third is different
Each axis has a unique foreshortening ratio

elimination_hints.hint: All three axes equally foreshortened defines isometric, not dimetric.
No foreshortening would mean a 1:1:1 scale on all axes, which doesn't correspond to any standard axonometric projection.
Each axis unique defines trimetric projection, not dimetric.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: From the figure showing isometric axes, what is the angle between the two axes that make 30° with the horizontal?
explanation: The X-axis (30° right of horizontal) and Y-axis (30° left of horizontal) are the two axes making 30° with the horizontal. The angle between them = 30° + 30° + 60° (gap at bottom) = 120°. This is shown by the purple arc in the diagram.
image_alt: Isometric axes showing Z vertical, X at 30° right, Y at 30° left, with 120° between each pair. Right panel shows isometric scale (0.816) vs natural scale.

elimination_hints.hint: 60° is the angle between an isometric axis and the horizontal, not between the two inclined axes.
90° is the orthographic angle. Isometric axes are at 120° apart.
150° is the angle between the Z-axis and one inclined axis measured on the far side, not between the two inclined axes.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: From the figure showing the isometric view of a rectangular block, the top face (EFGH) appears as which shape?
explanation: In the isometric view, the top face EFGH (which is actually rectangular) appears as a parallelogram. The 90° angles of the rectangle become 60° and 120° in the isometric projection.
image_alt: Isometric view of rectangular block with vertices A-H labeled, top face (blue) EFGH, front face (pink) ABFE, side face (gray) BCGF.

elimination_hints.hint: A rectangle has 90° angles. In isometric, these become 60° and 120°, so the shape is a parallelogram, not a rectangle.
A trapezoid has only one pair of parallel sides. The isometric top face has two pairs of parallel sides (a parallelogram).
A rhombus has all four sides equal. The top face has two different side lengths (L and W), so it's a parallelogram, not a rhombus.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: From the figure showing circles on three faces of an isometric cube, the ellipse on the front face (red) has its major axis oriented at approximately:
explanation: On the front face (XZ isometric plane), the major axis of the ellipse is perpendicular to the Y-axis direction. Since the Y-axis goes at 30° left, the perpendicular is at 60° from horizontal (tilting to the upper right).
image_alt: Isometric cube with ellipses on three visible faces: blue (top), red (front), green (side). Each ellipse has a different orientation.

elimination_hints.hint: Horizontal major axis belongs to the top face ellipse, not the front face.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing top face with front face

elimination_hints.hint: Vertical major axis doesn't correspond to any standard isometric face ellipse orientation.
30° is the isometric axis angle, not the major axis angle. The major axis is perpendicular to the 'missing' axis direction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing axis angle with major axis angle

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: From the figure showing a stepped block in isometric view, the step height can be determined as:
explanation: The total height is marked as 20 mm, and the block has two equal steps (base height 10 mm + step height 10 mm). The step portion rises from height 10 to height 20 along the Z-axis, giving a step height of 10 mm.
image_alt: Isometric view of stepped block: base 40×30×10, upper step 20×30×10. Total height 20mm, total length 40mm, width 30mm.

elimination_hints.hint: 20 mm is the total height of the stepped block, not the step height alone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using total height instead of step height

elimination_hints.hint: 30 mm is the width (depth) of the block, not the step height.
40 mm is the total length of the base, not a height dimension.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text:
Assertion (A): The isometric scale factor is √(2/3) ≈ 0.816.
Reason (R): In isometric projection, the body diagonal of a cube is perpendicular to the projection plane, causing each edge to be foreshortened by the factor √(2/3).
reason: In isometric projection, the body diagonal of a cube is perpendicular to the projection plane, causing each edge to be foreshortened by the factor √(2/3).
explanation: Both are true and R correctly explains A. The isometric projection views along the body diagonal, and the mathematical projection of each edge onto the projection plane gives the factor √(2/3).

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the body diagonal viewing direction is the geometric basis for the √(2/3) scale.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting viewing direction with scale

elimination_hints.hint: R is true — the body diagonal is indeed perpendicular to the projection plane in isometric.
A is true — √(2/3) ≈ 0.8165 is the well-known isometric scale factor.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text:
Assertion (A): In practice, isometric drawings (using true lengths) are preferred over isometric projections (using isometric scale).
Reason (R): Using true lengths simplifies construction while still producing a visually proportionate 3D representation.
assertion: In practice, isometric drawings (using true lengths) are preferred over isometric projections (using isometric scale).
reason: Using true lengths simplifies construction while still producing a visually proportionate 3D representation.
explanation: Both are true and R correctly explains A. Isometric drawings avoid the tedious multiplication by 0.816 for every measurement. The resulting view is proportionally identical (just uniformly larger), so it serves the same purpose.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the simplification of using true lengths is the practical reason for the preference.
R is true — true lengths do simplify the drawing process while maintaining visual proportionality.
A is true — isometric drawings are standard practice in engineering, using true lengths for convenience.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text:
Assertion (A): True angles (e.g., 90°) between edges are preserved in isometric projection.
Reason (R): Isometric projection equally foreshortens all three axes, maintaining proportional distances.
assertion: True angles (e.g., 90°) between edges are preserved in isometric projection.
reason: Isometric projection equally foreshortens all three axes, maintaining proportional distances.
explanation: A is false — angles are NOT preserved in isometric projection. A 90° angle appears as 60° or 120°. R is true — isometric does equally foreshorten all axes. But equal foreshortening preserves proportions of lengths, not angles.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: A is false — 90° angles become 60°/120° in isometric. Both cannot be true and R cannot explain a false assertion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting the false assertion about angle preservation

elimination_hints.hint: A is false. 'Both true' is not possible when A is false.
R is true — all three axes are equally foreshortened in isometric. The reason is correct even though the assertion is false.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text:
Assertion (A): Hidden lines are generally not shown in isometric views unless necessary for clarity.
Reason (R): Isometric views are pictorial representations meant for visual understanding, and hidden lines add unnecessary complexity.
assertion: Hidden lines are generally not shown in isometric views unless necessary for clarity.
reason: Isometric views are pictorial representations meant for visual understanding, and hidden lines add unnecessary complexity.
explanation: Both are true and R correctly explains A. Isometric views are meant to give a clear 3D picture. Hidden lines (dashed) clutter the view and are usually omitted. They are added only when essential to show a critical hidden feature.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the pictorial purpose of isometric views justifies omitting hidden lines.
R is true — isometric views are indeed pictorial representations for visual understanding.
elimination_hints.misconception: Rejecting the nature of isometric views

elimination_hints.hint: A is true — this is standard drafting convention for isometric views.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text:
Match the axonometric projection type with its foreshortening property:

Column A: (P) Isometric, (Q) Dimetric, (R) Trimetric, (S) Oblique
Column B: (1) All three axes equally foreshortened, (2) Two axes equally foreshortened, third different, (3) All three axes differently foreshortened, (4) One face shows true shape, receding axis is foreshortened
explanation: Isometric: all 3 equal (P-1). Dimetric: 2 equal, 1 different (Q-2). Trimetric: all 3 different (R-3). Oblique: one face true, receding foreshortened (S-4).

elimination_hints.hint: P-2 gives isometric two-axis foreshortening. Isometric is defined by ALL THREE axes being equally foreshortened.
Q-3 gives dimetric all-different. Dimetric has TWO equal + 1 different. All-different is trimetric.
R-4 gives trimetric one-face-true. One face true is oblique projection. Trimetric has all three axes differently foreshortened.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text:
Match the true shape with its appearance in isometric view:

Column A: (P) Square, (Q) Circle, (R) Rectangle (L ≠ W), (S) Right angle (90°)
Column B: (1) Ellipse, (2) Parallelogram (all sides equal), (3) 60° or 120°, (4) Parallelogram (unequal adjacent sides)
explanation: Square → Rhombus/parallelogram with all sides equal (P-2). Circle → Ellipse (Q-1). Rectangle → Parallelogram with unequal adjacent sides (R-4). Right angle → 60° or 120° (S-3).

elimination_hints.hint: P-1 gives square an ellipse. A square (flat face) appears as a parallelogram, not an ellipse. Ellipses represent circles.
R-3 gives rectangle 60°/120°. An angle becomes 60°/120°, but a rectangle (a shape) becomes a parallelogram.
P-4 gives square unequal-sided parallelogram. A square has all sides equal, so its isometric image also has equal sides.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that isometric preserves side equality

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In an isometric view, the two receding axes make 30° angles with the horizontal.
explanation: The two non-vertical isometric axes (X and Y) make 30° with the horizontal reference line. The third axis (Z) is vertical. This is a defining characteristic of isometric projection.

elimination_hints.hint: The 30° angle is a fundamental property of isometric axes. The three axes at 120° apart require the two non-vertical axes to be at 30° from horizontal.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: In an isometric view, only lines parallel to the isometric axes can be directly measured to scale.
explanation: Only isometric lines (parallel to the three axes) can be directly measured. Non-isometric lines (diagonals, inclined edges) are foreshortened differently and must be located by their endpoints.

elimination_hints.hint: Non-isometric lines have a different foreshortening ratio than the isometric scale. They cannot be measured directly; their endpoints must be located using isometric coordinates.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all lines are equally measurable

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: An isometric drawing of an object is larger than its isometric projection.
explanation: Isometric drawing uses true lengths (scale = 1.0), while isometric projection uses the isometric scale (≈ 0.816). Since 1.0 > 0.816, the isometric drawing is uniformly larger by a factor of 1/0.816 ≈ 1.22.

elimination_hints.hint: Isometric drawing: scale = 1.0. Isometric projection: scale = 0.816. Since 1.0 > 0.816, the drawing is larger than the projection.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Parallel lines in the object remain parallel in the isometric view.
explanation: Isometric projection is a parallel (not perspective) projection. Parallel lines in the object project as parallel lines in the isometric view. This is true for all parallel projections.

elimination_hints.hint: In parallel projections (including isometric), parallelism is always preserved. Only in perspective projection do parallel lines converge.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The isometric scale factor is √(2/3) which is approximately equal to _____.
text_with_blanks: The isometric scale factor is √(2/3) which is approximately equal to _____.
explanation: √(2/3) = √2/√3 = 1.414/1.732 = 0.8165 ≈ 0.816. This is the ratio by which all dimensions are reduced in true isometric projection.

elimination_hints.hint: 0.500 = 1/2. The isometric scale is √(2/3) ≈ 0.816, not 1/2.
0.707 = 1/√2. The isometric scale is √(2/3), not 1/√2.
1.000 means no reduction. This is the scale for isometric drawings, not isometric projections.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: The approximate method commonly used to draw an ellipse (representing a circle) in isometric view is called the _____ method.
text_with_blanks: The approximate method commonly used to draw an ellipse (representing a circle) in isometric view is called the _____ method.
explanation: The 4-center (four-center) method uses four arcs drawn from four different centers to approximate an ellipse. It is the standard method for drawing circles in isometric views.

elimination_hints.hint: The concentric circle method is used for drawing ellipses in general, but the specific isometric method is the 4-center method.
Oblong method is not a standard term for isometric circle construction.
The trammel method draws exact ellipses using a strip. The 4-center method is the approximate method specific to isometric drawing.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Based on the scenario, how many visible faces can the engineer show in a single isometric view?
scenario: A mechanical engineer needs to create an isometric drawing of a rectangular machine bracket. The bracket is 80 mm long, 50 mm wide, and 30 mm tall. The engineer wants to show maximum information in a single isometric view using true lengths (isometric drawing).
explanation: In a standard isometric view, a maximum of 3 faces of a rectangular object are visible: the top face, one side (front) face, and one end (right) face. The other 3 faces are hidden.

elimination_hints.hint: 1 face visible is a standard orthographic view, not an isometric view.
2 faces visible occurs in some oblique views. Standard isometric shows 3 faces.
6 faces means all faces visible. Opposite faces are always hidden. Maximum 3 faces visible.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Based on the scenario, what is the correct approach for drawing the cylindrical holes in isometric?
scenario: A designer is creating an isometric assembly drawing of a mounting plate with cylindrical holes. The plate is horizontal (on the top isometric plane), and the holes pass vertically through it. The true diameter of each hole is 20 mm.
explanation: Since the holes are on the top (horizontal) isometric plane, each hole appears as an ellipse in the isometric view. The 4-center approximate method is used to draw these ellipses. For isometric drawing (true lengths), the enclosing rhombus has sides = 20 mm.

options.text: Draw ellipses using the 4-center method on the top isometric plane
Draw perfect circles of 20 mm diameter
Draw ellipses with major axis vertical

elimination_hints.hint: Perfect circles would only be correct if the holes were on a plane parallel to the projection plane. On the top isometric plane, circles appear as ellipses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming circles stay circular in isometric

elimination_hints.hint: Squares approximate the enclosing shape but don't represent circular holes. Ellipses are needed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using squares instead of ellipses for circles

elimination_hints.hint: Ellipses on the top face have a horizontal major axis, not vertical. Vertical major axis corresponds to side faces.
elimination_hints.misconception: Wrong major axis orientation for top face

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Arrange the steps for drawing an isometric view of a rectangular block from given orthographic views:

items.text: Study the orthographic views and identify the length (L), width (W), and height (H)
Draw the three isometric axes (vertical + two at 30° to horizontal)
Mark L along one axis, W along the other, and H along the vertical axis
Complete the isometric box by drawing parallel lines from each endpoint
Darken the visible edges and show hidden edges as dashed lines (if needed)

explanation: Correct order: Read ortho views → Draw axes → Mark dimensions → Complete the box → Darken/hide edges → Add dimensions.

elimination_hints.hint: Step 2 (draw axes) before Step 1 (study views) means drawing without knowing the dimensions. Always analyze first.
Step 3 (mark dimensions) before Step 2 (draw axes) is impossible. You need axes to mark dimensions on.
Step 4 (complete box) before Step 3 (mark dimensions) won't work. The box needs the dimension endpoints.

subject: engineering-graphics
question_text: Arrange the steps for drawing an isometric ellipse using the 4-center approximate method on the top face:

items.text: Draw the isometric rhombus (parallelogram) enclosing the circle on the top isometric plane
Find the midpoints of all four sides of the rhombus
Draw perpendicular bisectors from the midpoints of the shorter diagonal's sides toward the obtuse angle corners
Identify the four centers: two at the obtuse angle corners and two at the intersection of perpendicular bisectors
Draw two large arcs from the obtuse-angle centers passing through adjacent midpoints
Draw two small arcs from the other two centers to complete the ellipse

explanation: Correct order: Draw rhombus → Find midpoints → Draw perpendicular bisectors → Identify 4 centers → Draw large arcs → Draw small arcs to complete.

elimination_hints.hint: Step 3 (perpendicular bisectors) before Step 2 (finding midpoints) won't work. Bisectors originate from midpoints.
Step 2 (midpoints) before Step 1 (rhombus) is impossible. You need the rhombus sides to find their midpoints.
Step 4 (identify centers) before Step 3 (draw bisectors) won't work. Two of the four centers are found by the bisector intersections.
